prompt: Write about a recent retreat or getaway where you were able to focus on writing or another creative pursuit.
reference:
•5:00-7:00 p.m. Fresh air, walking, and creative well refilling. Whether that was strolling through an art gallery, walking around looking at historic buildings, churches, and sculptures - it was a brain break.

•7:00-8:30 p.m. Dinner. Naturally, we'd end up talking about our work, which led to life, family, and all the things business friends talk about.

•9:00-10:00 p.m. Relax, unwind, then bed.

I didn't have a word count goal going into the retreat, but I had about six ideas bulleted out that I needed to flesh out. I guessed that each of the six ideas would be roughly the length of a blog post. It turns out I was right.

•After day one, hour one, I had written 3,557 words (which counted the bullets I had outlined ahead of time). My most productive session!

•By the end of day one, I had 4,700 words.

•By 10:00 a.m. on day two, I had 5,680 words. And I spent much of the second day revisiting what I wrote on day one, which meant tweaking and adding stuff.

•By 11:00 a.m. (lunchtime), I had 6,231 words.

•On day three, I had a three-hour writing marathon in the middle of the day for my community (which meant they were writing and I was coaching) and had to scoot out early to get to my son's playoff hockey game, so I didn't write as much as I thought I would.

•Total three-day writing time for my business: eleven hours.

•Total new words for my business: 8,200 words.

When I first saw the total word count and time invested for three days, my ego wanted to feel crappy about it - I should have prepared more, pushed harder, and written more.

But I stopped that mind chatter. I did more than enough. At this time, I wasn't yet aligning my writing activities with nature's rhythms. We had some great conversations, and I had to think through some business decisions as these came up in my writing. And there were 8,200 words to use in my business that didn't exist before.

Here's an estimated breakdown of my total costs:

•Airbnb for three nights (my portion): $260

•Gas: $30

•Parking: $100

•Lunches and dinners: $400 (this was the highest because I like food and picked up the tab for at least one dinner)

Total cost: $790.

You could do it for much more or much less. Travel costs were low, and food costs were relatively higher.

My biggest worry going into this writing retreat: If I was investing all this time and energy in this trip, I wanted to be sure I'd get more writing done than I would have at home.

Since I didn't plan much in advance, I worried that I'd have too much client work to do or that I wouldn't feel inspired, clear, or even have the stamina to write anything useful. Next time, I'll plan it further in advance and make sure to block my calendar, so I have complete focus.

One of us didn't write for the specific project we were working on at all on day one - and that's okay.

After our second Pomodoro sprint, as I was pouring a third cup of coffee (the cups were tiny), one friend looked up from her laptop and said, "I'm not writing what I'm supposed to."

Without missing a beat, I replied, "That's okay." Because it is okay. She was writing something - that's the important part. She was journaling, emailing, and doing other writing.

It was so powerful to see living proof that my belief that writer's block is BS was accurate. She had some stuff to sort out in her mind and heart before she could get to her intended project.

By the afternoon, she was ready to work on her project, and by day two, she wrote like an intuitive MOFO.

I hope this inspires you to create a little writing retreat of your own - to start your website, a book, a screenplay, a few months of blog content, a bunch of poems, or whatever it is you want to write.

Extended vacations can turn into a writing retreat. I've kick-started lots of projects on planes sans Wi-Fi. You'll love how Mairéad, a writing community member, turned travel into a daily writing habit.

Before 2020, we used to travel for months from Ireland to mainland Europe in our camper van. I wrote a blog post most days. I cannot understand where I found the time. Travel in a van has lots of extra chores because it's not connected to water or electricity, it has no dishwasher or washing machine, and on damp days there's no suitable place to hang your wet clothes. It can get very uncomfortable.

fine_tuned.response:
A week or so ago, I went back out for a quick 4-day writing retreat in Philly with two business friends. We were looking forward to some dedicated time together and having each other's backs as we worked on our writing projects.

Before leaving, I made sure to get as much work done for my one-on-one copywriting clients as I could in advance. This way, I could focus on getting some serious writing done while away from the daily responsibilities of running a household. One friend had a pretty solid draft already going into the retreat and was worried she wouldn't be able to keep up - I know how that feels!

We rented a great short-term condo with plenty of space for each of us to write, plus walking distance to restaurants so we didn't have to cook or clean up after ourselves. It was perfect! We met in advance on two calls to discuss our writing plans and outlines, which really helped everyone get focused.

I was feeling a little anxious about being ready - I had my 2-page super rough bulleted outline all ready to go - but having some dedicated time with friends turned out to be just what I needed. Having each other's backs (and good vibes) made it easy for us to stay motivated and inspired throughout the retreat.

In between writing sessions, we'd take breaks and explore the city together. It was amazing how much inspiration came from seeing new things and putting ourselves in different environments - especially with like-minded people.

It really hit home how much I love working in a group - when it's intentional and focused. We were all on the same team and had shared goals, making it easy to work together, bounce ideas off each other, and make progress.

Now that we're back home, we've got some great writing projects under way. I feel like our retreat helped us stay focused and inspired. And even though life has been crazy lately (who hasn't experienced that?), we had a clear plan for what we wanted to accomplish together while on the retreat. Having a plan - no matter how simple - can go a long way in keeping you motivated and feeling accomplished.

I'm so grateful for this experience, not just because it felt like a much-needed break from my daily life (and I got some great writing done), but also because it was amazing to see how we could support each other through our shared goals. We were all working together toward something - no distractions or outside influences pulling us apart.

It's funny when you're in the middle of your day-to-day life and get stuck on a project that doesn't feel like fun anymore, and all you need is some time to focus, take a break from the world (and others), and come back with fresh eyes. That's exactly what this retreat allowed me to do.

We went into it ready for work - no messing around or wasting time. And when we left, we felt refreshed and ready to go. It was just like having a clear plan helped us stay focused. We didn't have any distractions (no phone, no TV, no social media), so the only thing on our minds were our writing projects.

I think this is something that everyone can learn from - you don't need to be in a fancy retreat or expensive resort. You just need some dedicated time with clear intentions and a supportive team around you.

As I look back on what we did, I realize how much more we could do together. And it's not just about writing - we could work on any project that requires focus. The key to success is having a plan, staying focused, and supporting each other through our shared goals.

It really makes me think - if you're feeling stuck on your projects or overwhelmed with all the things you need to write, maybe you can create your own little retreat in your day-to-day life too. What would that look like for you? How could you set yourself up to be successful and feel supported?

I highly recommend a writing retreat. If you haven't had one yet, I hope this gives you some inspiration - or if you're feeling stuck on what to do with yours.

baseline.response:
That weekend in Big Sur - I still feel it coursing through my veins like a rich red wine. My husband and the kids had flown out for a family camp adventure, leaving me with the perfect excuse to slip away from daily life. I packed up my trusty laptop, some scribbled notes, and an embarrassingly large collection of tea bags (because you never know when inspiration strikes).

I settled into a cozy cabin perched on the edge of a towering redwood forest. The scent of damp earth and eucalyptus wafted through the windows as I poured myself a steaming cup - just that. No fancy coffee shop, no pretentious pour-over, just good old-fashioned simplicity.

For three blissful days, I wrote without checking my phone or email. No social media to distract me from the rustle of leaves outside. Just the hum of silence and the occasional hoot of an owl. And what did I create? Not much, actually - but that was the point. I showed up with a vague idea, no outline, and a willingness to listen.

I spent hours scribbling in my journal, letting the words spill out without editing or censoring myself. It was like giving myself permission to be messy, just as the forest was messy - full of dead leaves, twisted branches, and hidden clearings. And then, something miraculous happened: I started to see patterns emerge from the chaos.

Not even close to perfect writing, but it felt true. The kind of truth that comes from taking a leap, trusting your intuition, and letting go of expectations. When did we women last do that? When did we last give ourselves permission to make art without apology?

Now I'm back home, surrounded by my family's energetic chaos - and it's time to integrate the lessons from Big Sur into my daily life. Can you imagine what would happen if we all took a few hours each week to listen, to let go, and to create without expectation? Sound familiar?

prompt: Write a blog post titled "Jacqueline Fisch - Wishbeads Guest Blog post"
reference:
I've been a long-time fan of Wishbeads bracelets. First, and selfishly, for myself - I mean, who wouldn't want another tool to help make their wishes come true?

And then, as a writer and writing coach, I wanted to keep finding ways to surprise and delight my clients. I mean, I can only send people so many pens and notebooks and what better way to celebrate the work my clients accomplish than to support them in making their wishes come true.

What's even more amazing is hearing the stories and seeing the pictures of the beads on people's social media feed. My clients often become my friends, and it's incredibly satisfying to deepen a friendship by honoring each other's wishes. I keep a few dozen Wishbeads in a variety of colors stashed under my desk to surprise and delight my clients.

As I package them up, gratitude and a knowing smile always wash over me as I imagine the look on their face, and then them making a wish, rolling it up, and slipping it inside the secret compartment. And of course, when their dreams become a reality.

Swapping out my wishpaper when my wish has come true brings me tremendous joy.

I have a few Wishbeads bracelets, and they all contain a different wish. There's something that happens when you intentionally focus on a specific outcome - you notice all the ways your wish is already on its way to you.

What's also beautiful is how your wish might show up in a slightly different form. It's an excellent reminder to let go of the how. I realized along this wishing journey, that the more I pay attention and look for signs of my wish out there, the faster wishes come true. There's actually some brain science to this too - it's called the reticular activating system (or the RAS) and our brain shows us what we're focusing on. Same reason that once you decide to go Jeep shopping you can't help but notice all the Jeeps on the road.

This past summer, I wanted to give the ladies in my mastermind group a special gift - something we could all connect with, and deepen our relationship. Having shared many hours of conversations, connections, tears, and advice with them over the past year, I wanted to gift them something meaningful.

I'm also unreasonably practical, so the idea of giving a gift that sits on a shelf or doesn't get used hurts my heart a little.

Inside our mastermind group, we all have big goals and important dreams.

What if we all had Wishbeads to cement our highest hopes for ourselves and our businesses?

Wanting to give each of these important women a color they'd love, I sent everyone a cryptic message the evening before my flight to meet them at our annual retreat, "Black, blue, or green?" The three colors of bracelets I had beneath my desk. I collected their responses, tucked their Wishbeads into my carry-on bag and boarded the plane.

Our theme for our most recent group mastermind retreat was rejuvenation, and we had just spent a delicious hour vibrating along with a sound bath experience. It was my first sound bath experience - you lay down on yoga mats while someone creates sounds using a variety of instruments, including tuning forks, gongs, crystal singing bowls, chimes, and voice.

The sound bath left us deeply relaxed, so now was the perfect time to present the gifts.

I called each member of our group over and presented a Wishbeads bracelet in their chosen color, which was followed by gasps of delight, gratitude, and hugs. Having been an experienced "wisher," I offered up some suggestions on the specific language to use when writing their wishes.

The results of our group all leaving that evening, making wishes, and seeing them wearing their intentions was potent.

Some ladies shared their wishes later with the group, and some kept them private.

Within a few weeks the updates started rolling in:

My wish came true already!

I crushed it! Time for a new wish!

My wish is coming true!

It ended up looking slightly different, but still amazing!

Wishing as a group was a powerful and moving experience. In our inner circle, we held each other accountable and were able to hold the space, and in a way, amplify one another's wishes. Having insight into each other's big intentions also helped us to serve as reminders for one another - for those of us who shared our wish, we now had five other women keeping their eyes and ears open for clues.

Wishing alone can take you far, wishing together can take you farther!

Wishbeads Guest Blog Post: Jacqueline Fisch on Intuitive Writing

I'm so excited to be here today at the Wishbeads website sharing some of my favorite tips for intuitive writing. I've been playing with words in this way since 2012 (my vegan food blog days) and have learned a thing or two along the way.

Intuitive writing is all about tuning into your inner voice, going with your gut, and letting go of overthinking. It's how I write my blog posts each week - by jumping in without a plan.

When you start writing from an intuitive place, you'll find that words come more easily to you. You won't feel stuck or forced to say something specific (like trying to fit 800 words into your blog post). And the best part? Your readers will respond to it! They can sense when someone is being genuine and real.

This past year, I've been working on my own writing projects - books, online courses, blogs. I've had some amazing experiences along the way (and some pretty cringeworthy moments too) - but one thing that's helped me stay consistent with my creative output has been intuitive writing. By doing so, it feels like what I write is "right" for me.

So how do you start playing with intuitive writing? Here are a few simple tips:

Start by taking pen to paper and just writing something. It can be your morning pages (Julia Cameron style) or a quick freewrite in a Google Doc. Just let the words flow out of you without thinking too much about them.

Read back what you've written and see if there's anything that stands out. You might have some great insights, ideas, or even entire sections come to life. That was one of my favorite parts of playing with intuitive writing - it felt like I'd uncovered something amazing!

Let your intuition guide you as you write. If a sentence doesn't feel right, keep going! Sometimes we need to go through the motions and get out all our thoughts so that we can see what's really important to us.

I love this quote from Julia Cameron, "The Artist's Way" - "Your job isn't to be brilliant or talented; it's only to show up day after day." That's been my experience with intuitive writing. I don't need to have a plan for each blog post, but instead, I can just trust that the words will come.

Intuitive writing is all about having fun and trusting your inner guidance (and giving yourself permission to be imperfect). It's also about learning to listen to what wants to come through you - not forcing it out. Let me know if you have any other questions or if you'd like some suggestions for how to start playing with intuitive writing in your own life.

Happy writing!

**The Intentional Writer**

I'm Jacqueline Fisch, and I'm thrilled to be here on Wishbeads today. As a writer, writing coach, and host of the podcast "How Women Write," I've had the privilege of exploring what it means to write with intention – not just with pen in hand, but with a deep understanding of ourselves.

I still remember when I first started my writing journey. I was a corporate professional by day, and a wannabe writer at night. I'd sit at my desk, staring blankly at the computer screen, wondering why everything I wrote felt so... forced. Like I was trying to cram the words into neat little boxes instead of letting them flow freely.

Sound familiar?

It wasn't until I stumbled upon Elizabeth Gilbert's Big Magic that I realized the problem wasn't me – it was my approach. I'd been trying to write like everyone else, not trusting my own voice or intuition. It was like attempting to brew a cup of tea without ever tasting it first; you end up with a bitter mess.

So, I started small. I began writing morning pages (Julia Cameron's genius idea). Just 10-15 minutes a day, scribbling whatever came to mind. No editing, no censoring – just me and the paper. And you know what? It changed everything.

I started trusting my voice again. I discovered that my writing wasn't about perfection; it was about showing up, honestly, with each new page. Not even close.

And here's the thing: this isn't just about writing. It's about living – with intention, with presence, with trust in ourselves. We can apply these same principles to our daily lives, not just our writing desks.

So, what if you just... stopped trying to fix it? What if you let your words flow like a river, without judgment or expectation?

For me, that's been the key to finding my creative voice – and my authentic self. I'm still a work in progress (every single time), but at least now I know where to start.

**Reflection:** Take 10 minutes today to write without editing. Just let your words flow – no matter how messy they get. See what happens.

prompt: Describe the writing process as a journey of intentional motion and growth, where one's body of work is carefully curated to nourish and delight their audience.
reference:
An object in motion tends to stay in motion. That's our goal here - motion. And not just any kind of motion: Intentional, grounded, and aligned motion to support your highest path. There will be bumps and it will be uncomfortable at times. The more you embrace discomfort and feel okay in it, the more you'll grow as a writer and as a person. Buckle up and enjoy the ride.

Chapter 20: Your body of work

In Chapter 13 we talked about your charcuterie board of content. If you're worried your content, books, and blog posts all read like a mishmash of topics, I invite you to think about everything you write about as a component that goes on one of these tastebud adventures on a wooden plank.

The tastes of the charcuterie board are delicious when enjoyed alone and even better when paired together. And with a delightfully curated presentation, it's also a feast for your eyes.

Applying the charcuterie concept to your writing, each of your blog posts, courses, and books are nourishing and helpful on its own. When enjoyed one after another with a small taste from all the other accompanying content - the experience is even better.

Your group programs paired with your weekly emails.

Your books paired with your newsletter.

Your social media captions paired with your poetry.

Your podcast paired with your workshops.

This is why organizing your content into buckets, or a handful of key themes, will help your work appear as a charcuterie board. You wouldn't drop mashed potatoes, Brussels sprouts, or spaghetti on your board. It wouldn't fit.

And now, if you're wondering how many themes you have or how even to begin organizing your body of work, here are some ways to figure them out.

Remember that each piece of writing you create is not for everyone. Think about the writers you enjoy - do you absolutely love every bit of their work? Maybe. But chances are that you enjoy some pieces more than others.

Just like the plant-based eater at your party, they're not going to be mad if there's prosciutto on your plate - they'll just eat the things they prefer.

1. Scan your existing writing.

Look across everything you've ever written. If you had to put all the topics into categories, where would they naturally fit? Then, consider your list of groups and think about combining or removing some if there are too many. Are there some topics you no longer write about? You can let those live on the internet and write about things you're most interested in now. If you notice you have a single theme, that's great too. You can absolutely stick to one subject or add more if you feel inspired in the months and years ahead.

2. Consider what you want to be known for.

How do you want people to remember you? Do you want them to remember you for your ability to tell a story and apply a life lesson? Do you want to be known for your no-BS talk? What subject matter do you want readers to associate with your name? Do you want to be a poet who cooks? A romance writer who's also a kickass marketer?

3. Consider what you could talk about all day long.

When you talk about something, and it brings you an inner feeling of excitement and enthusiasm, that right there is magic - follow it. Remember, what feels good to you to create feels good to your readers. List out all the topics that make you feel antsy with anticipation. Subjects so good that you can't wait to share what you know.

4. What do people always ask for your advice on?

If people constantly ask you for cooking advice and you're a life coach, you may want to keep those topics to a minimum. When you notice you're giving the same advice repeatedly, you've got a gift right there. That's the magic sauce you can serve to your audience.

Intuitive Writing Tip

Uncouple the idea that everything you write needs to make money. Some writing can (and should) be for fun or for helping others. Let your art be art without putting pressure on it to do something for you - like make you famous or rake in a million bucks.

5. Write out your list of themes - maybe you have one, three, or even six.

Review each topic on the list. Do you enjoy and want to continue talking about these topics? If not, go ahead and strike some. Maybe what you remove from your blog content is what you'll share in your books. And if you do love these topics, now you have some boundaries to help you brainstorm.

I've written about 1,000 words on this topic.

I don't believe in having a block or being stuck. I think it's all about getting into flow state - which means that you're fully immersed and enjoying the process of creating something. And that's what writing is for me. It's a creative activity when I'm in flow.

So, how do I get into flow? Well, I let go of my ego. I surrender to the process. When I write from a meditative state with my eyes closed, I know that I'm only a channel for what wants to come through - not trying to control it or force it, but just letting it flow.

And then, when I'm in flow, I don't think about editing or even writing itself. I just start writing and let the words out. And then later on, I'll go back and edit only what's needed for clarity and readability - not trying to change everything, just making sure that the message comes through clearly.

This is how you can unlock a deep flow state in your own work as well. By surrendering to the process and letting it come through naturally, without ego or forcing it, you'll be able to tap into your true creative potential. It's what I call "intuitive writing" - trusting that whatever comes out on the page is exactly what needs to be written.

And when we're in flow, time doesn't exist. We're completely immersed and enjoying the process of creating something new. Which means that our body of work will naturally flow from one project to another. It's like having a charcuterie board of content where each piece feeds into the other. And it feels so good.

For me, this journey of intentional motion and growth is about trusting my intuition and surrendering to what wants to come through. I don't have a plan or an agenda - only a willingness to create something new and let go of what's been holding me back.

baseline.response:
The writing process - it's like navigating a winding road on a summer morning, with the sun rising over the hills, casting a warm glow over everything. You're not just getting from point A to B, you're savoring the view, feeling the breeze in your hair, and listening to the songs of the birds.

For me, writing is about moving intentionally through the terrain of my own creativity - not just churning out words for the sake of it, but cultivating a body of work that's like a garden: tended with care, pruned with discipline, and ultimately, a place where beauty blooms.

I used to think that writing was about producing a certain number of words per day, or meeting some arbitrary deadline. But now I see it as an invitation to move deeper into myself - to listen to the whispers of my intuition, to trust in the flow of ideas, and to let go of the need for control.

And just like any journey worth taking, there are detours and surprises along the way. Some days, the words flow effortlessly, like a gentle stream babbling over smooth stones. Other days, it's more like navigating a muddy trail - you're slogging through thick underbrush, pushing aside branches that seem to block your path.

But here's the thing: every step of the journey is an opportunity for growth. Every sentence written, every paragraph revised, every manuscript edited is a chance to refine my craft, to hone my voice, and to connect with others on a deeper level.

As I write, I'm not just crafting words - I'm cultivating relationships. With my readers, yes, but also with myself. I'm learning to listen more deeply, to trust my instincts, and to surrender to the process.

And what emerges from this journey? A body of work that's like a curated collection of treasures: each piece unique, yet connected in some hidden way; each one a reflection of the person I'm becoming, with all her quirks and flaws.

So if you're on your own writing journey - don't worry about the destination. Focus on the movement itself. Pay attention to the language of your soul, and let it guide you forward. And remember: every step, no matter how small, is a step closer to creating something that nourishes and delights your audience.

What if you just... stopped trying to fix it? What if instead, you allowed yourself to flow with the current of your own creativity, trusting that the river will carry you where you need to go?

prompt: Describe the strategies you use to manage your thoughts and emotions when faced with negative self-talk or criticism from others.
reference:
One of my favorites is the cancel process. While I'm usually thoughtful with my words, sometimes my mouth moves faster than my brain, and I say something stupid or out of line. Wait! I take that back! Well, I do the same with my thinking. When a thought creeps up that dulls my shine, I say something in my head like cancel, cancel, cancel, or delete, delete, delete, or reject and delete.

Then, I get to tell a better story.

You are in charge of your thoughts. You can change them whenever you like. Hear a load of crap in your ear? Stop that thought and tell yourself another one. You don't have to be all fairy dust about it and tell yourself that you're a sparkly rainbow of luminous delight. Use words you'd use with close friends, like "Hey, this is completely normal, do better next time."

I also have a thank bank that I deposit praise, kind emails, and kudos into regularly. I keep mine in a Google Doc and in a special email folder that I literally named "Thank Bank." Every time I get a gushing email, or a small note of thanks or praise, I take a copy and store it in my thank bank. On days when my thoughts are running wild, I make a mistake, or actually do suck at something, I read through my thank bank and feel so much better.

Beyond showering myself in gratitude, sharing gratitude outward gives me an instant boost. Sometimes I write a thank-you note on a card and drop it in the mail, send a nice email, or write a review of a favorite restaurant.

In addition, consider getting a coach, a therapist, and some friends who will check your limiting thoughts at the door. Even better, get all three - remember, you become the essence of the five people you spend the most time with.

For most of my life I tried to go it alone. The lone warrior, I always had an idea that asking for help made me weak. I'm still unraveling those behaviors. The more you ask for help, the more you realize that people want to help. Especially when those people are good at it.

Here's what my support network looks like these days - Ryan holding up the other end of our household in his career, striving for more and better, and pushing me to be better. My kids to show me where I need to work on myself, and for them to do chores around the house (for this reason, a housekeeper is not yet part of my support system). A paid mastermind because I don't want to be the smartest person in a room. Business besties - friends I can text in the middle of the day when I'm stuck or need to bounce my latest idea off someone. A coach - who's supported me in wiping out negative emotions (yeah, I didn't think it was possible either) and showing me how to train my brain to expect good things. My work with my coach has been a game changer. My family, who I can always count on. Even though we're five hundred miles away, they're my number one fans.

I have to tell you that I'm pretty sensitive to my state of being - how I'm feeling at any given moment. And if someone says something that's going to make me feel crappy, I need to be super mindful about what I do immediately after.

First, I get quiet and listen to myself. This is key. Because the more you pay attention to your inner voice, the less you'll hear external voices - critics, naysayers, or even yourself. If someone says something that triggers a negative state in me, my first move is to notice how I'm feeling. And then, I take action quickly.

I might say out loud, "Cancel, cancel, cancel" - like I'm deleting the thought from my mind. Or, if it's something more serious, I'll ask myself, "What story am I telling about this situation?" Often when we're in a negative state, it's because our thinking is stuck on a particular narrative or belief.

I also have a great practice that helps me manage my thoughts and emotions: morning pages. This is a technique that Julia Cameron recommends, where you write down your thoughts first thing in the morning - whatever comes to mind without judgment or editing. It's like clearing out all the mental clutter and getting clear about what's going on.

And if I find myself stuck in a negative state for an extended period of time, I know it's time to get curious. What am I avoiding? Why do I feel this way? And then, I take action - whether that means talking to someone, changing my environment, or simply doing something else.

I also use gratitude to shift my state. When you focus on what you're grateful for, your brain doesn't have space for negative thoughts. I keep a "Thank Bank" where I store all the positive notes and praise from people - it's amazing how that can turn my day around!

Ultimately, managing our thoughts and emotions is an ongoing process. It takes practice, but with awareness and intention, we can choose to change our state in any moment.

And remember, you have the power to interrupt a negative state at any time. When someone criticizes me or I'm feeling down, it's not that I don't care about what they're saying - I just need to take control of how I feel and say, "No, no, no. I cancel this thought." And then, I choose something else.

It might sound simple, but managing your state is like keeping a garden. You have to tend to it every day so that you can be the best version of yourself - one thought at a time.

The constant companion of self-doubt – it's a real party crasher, isn't it? But here's the thing: I've learned to invite it in, get cozy with it, even make friends with it.

When that inner critic starts piping up, telling me I'm not good enough or that my writing is crap, I take a deep breath and say – honestly, out loud – "Thanks for showing up, self-doubt. But you're just here to keep things interesting." 

And then I get curious. What's driving this negativity? Is it fear of failure? Fear of being seen? Fear that my writing isn't good enough (oh, the irony)? Whatever it is, I start to explore it, to understand where these thoughts are coming from.

Now, when someone else comes along and tries to tear me down – which, let's be real, happens more often than I'd like – I've got a few tricks up my sleeve. First, I remind myself that their words are a reflection of them, not me. They're probably struggling with their own demons, and projecting onto me is an easy way out.

But here's the key: I don't internalize it. I don't let their criticism seep into my bones and make me doubt everything I've worked for. Instead, I take a step back, breathe in some fresh air (or a glass of wine, depending on the situation), and remind myself why I started writing in the first place.

It's not about being perfect; it's about showing up with intention, with heart, and with a willingness to learn. So when someone tries to bring me down, I say – firmly but kindly – "Thanks for your input. But my worth isn't defined by your opinion."

And that, friends, is the magic of reframing criticism as feedback. It's not about silencing our critics; it's about learning from them, and using their words to fuel our own growth.

So what if you just... stopped trying to fix yourself based on others' opinions? What would happen if you started showing up for yourself, with kindness, compassion, and a willingness to learn?

I first got that little itch to start my own business in 2009 (eight years ago from this writing). My son was a year old, and I had just been laid off for the first time (turned out it would happen twice more). Except I had no ideas, so I got another job.

Again in 2011, while pregnant with my daughter, I came across a book on my lunch hour, called the Firestarter Sessions. It was all about finding work that you love. The kind of work you were meant to do.

Oh - this is a thing? I don't need to have a job? I can create work that's perfect for me? I had no idea.

I dug into those worksheets like my life depended on it. I wasn't sure what to do. I started a food and lifestyle blog.

Five years later, still brainstorming, still pouring over the worksheets, wondering what that work was that I was supposed to do, it finally hit me - I want to write. I want to write more for myself and others. I had written a book a few years prior; I wanted to do more. And I wanted to write for other people. I was doing in a corporate setting, but I needed more control over my time and the work I took on.

My point - keep trying different ideas. There's insight inside every action.

Here's a list of all the things I tried to turn into a business:

Food blogging

Cookbook writing

Cooking classes

Private cooking lessons

Customized meal plans based on people's taste buds

Minimalism coaching

Accountability coaching

If I didn't try all these things, I might not have landed on what I'm doing today. The side hustle that turned into a profitable business and replaced my corporate income might have never happened, and I'd still be commuting three hours a day and rushing around like a frazzled mom trying to keep everything together.

If you're sitting there reading this and are thinking that you want to do something "else," and you just don't know what that something else is just yet, just keep looking.

Reading some self-help books and doing some workbooks can help, but there are only so many online courses and experts you can listen to. You need to listen to the most important expert - you.

Try one thing. Do it until it doesn't work anymore, or make tweaks and keep plugging on. Keep doing that until you find work that feels good.

Get out of your head and off the couch, and out from behind the laptop. The only way you'll know is if you take action and give things a try. Hey, you might land on something that really lights your fire right away, or maybe it will take 30 tries. Maybe you'll never land on one thing; you'll land on doing a few things.

fine_tuned.response:
Finding Your Way: An Intuitive Approach to Navigating Uncertainty

You know what uncertainty feels like.

It's when you're sitting in your home, staring at a blank wall (or screen), and have no idea how to start making progress on that thing you've been meaning to do for a while.

Maybe it's starting a new business. Or launching an online course. Writing a book. Building a community around your passion project.

Perhaps you're not even sure what that "thing" is. You just know that you don't feel satisfied with where you are right now. And you have this nagging feeling that there's more out there for you.

Whatever it might be, uncertainty can be uncomfortable and frustrating.

And often, we try to figure things out in our heads first. We think about it, plan it, make lists, break it down into tiny steps... But sometimes even with all the planning, preparation, and execution - you still get stuck.

It's like trying to force a square peg into a round hole. It might look good on paper, but when you try to implement it in real life, it just doesn't fit.

In our "How Women Write" community, we talk about intuitive writing all the time (it's one of my favorite topics!). We discuss how writing from a place of intuition can feel more like movement than force. It feels like dancing or painting - not forcing your body to do something.

Intuitive writing is not just for writers. It applies to every area of life where you need creative problem-solving and guidance.

So, if the uncertainty is feeling overwhelming, here are some steps to try on:

1. Ask yourself: What's my greatest desire? If I had all the time in the world and no limits on resources - what would I do?

2. Go back to your childhood dreams. What did you want to be when you grew up? Before you knew it was "impossible" or too expensive.

3. Think about how you feel when you're fully immersed in something that brings you joy. What are the emotions associated with that feeling? Is that something you've been neglecting?

4. Ask someone who knows you well: What do they see as your greatest strength? What talents or skills come most naturally to you? How can you combine those things?

5. When you're ready, take a step.

And then another one.

Keep going until you feel like you're moving forward - not necessarily because you have an end goal in mind but because you enjoy the process.

You'll know when you're finding your way. It might look different than what you expected. And that's okay.

The point is to start moving and trust that you have a direction, even if it feels fuzzy at first.

And remember - consistency beats perfection every time. You don't need to have everything figured out before taking action. Just show up, do your best, and be willing to change course when needed.

You're not alone in this journey. We all find our way through uncertainty. It's a natural part of the process of growth and transformation.

And if you ever get stuck - take a break, go outside, breathe some fresh air, and come back to it when you feel ready again.

That's how we move forward with purpose and intention - even in the face of uncertainty.

How do you find your way? Do you have any strategies that you use when faced with uncertainty? I'd love to hear them!

prompt: Write about why women should stop waiting for permission to write
reference:
Here's what nobody tells you about writing — you don't need permission.

Not from your partner. Not from your kids. Not from that voice in your head that says you should be doing laundry instead.

You just need to sit down and do it.

I know, I know. Easier said than done. But here's the thing — every woman I've coached, every single one, has said some version of "I'll start writing when..." When the kids are older. When work slows down. When I have something worth saying.

Screw that.

You have something worth saying right now. Today. This minute.

And I'm not talking about some grand literary masterpiece. I'm talking about the stuff that's been rattling around in your head at 2am. The stories you tell your best friend over wine. The observations that make you stop mid-walk and think — damn, that's something.

That's your writing. It already exists.

So why are you waiting? Who exactly is supposed to hand you a golden ticket that says "You May Now Write"? Because I'll tell you — that person doesn't exist. She never did.

The permission slip is yours to write. Always has been.

Start messy. Start scared. Start with ten minutes before the house wakes up.

Just start.

Some people even think it's permission from their parents, a spouse, or their boss. But really, you don't need anyone's permission to write. You only need your own.

The problem with writing is that we often see the work as a performance - something that needs to be "good enough" to go out into the world and make us feel seen. And when it comes to our creative work, we're so worried about how others will perceive it that we stall on letting it happen.

We think, "Well, I need to take this class first," or "I have to read more books in my genre." Or even, "I just want to be sure that someone out there will like what I write."

And all of these thoughts are valid. But they're not necessary for starting the work.

Once you start writing, you'll find that the work will reveal itself. The drafts that sit around in your computer files for years - the ones you're afraid to share with anyone - they're just waiting.

They need air.

They need a container.

A space to be seen without judgment.

And it's only when we write without permission that we get real, raw, and honest about our thoughts and ideas. When we let go of needing validation from others, we can start writing for ourselves. For the joy of creation, not just for external praise or personal gain.

When you stop waiting for permission to write...

You're opening yourself up to more - more freedom, more authenticity, more connection with your readers.

The work will be there when you show up.

It's been showing up all along.

And it's only by letting go of the need for validation that we can actually share our true voice and let our writing flow.

prompt: Write a blog post about crisis comms
reference:
## things I learned from working in crisis communications for three years

From 2009 until 2012 I worked for a major telecommunications company. BlackBerry, maybe you've heard of them? Back then, they were called RIM. Yes, I had RIM-job (had to).

Even though most of us are wielding iPhones now... you know from the other fruit company (not me though, Android all the way because Apple was the enemy back in those BlueBerry days.

Back in 2009 though, BlackBerry was in its heyday. My first job interviews them was the day after their private Van Halen concert. Well, shit, this company sounds pretty cool.

I landed my RIM-job shortly after getting laid off for the first time from a Chicago-based management consulting company. I am grateful for everything the company did... like relocated our family of three at the time from Chicago to the Greater Toronto Area and made sure everything was smooth.

This was my first formal communications job. Sure, I had written tons up until this point. Proposals worth millions of dollars, emails worth even more, but they had rules and process, and it was there that I really started paying attention to word choice.

The experiences I had at BlackBerry serve me today. When you mess up and send the wrong thing to the wrong client when poo flies sideways, and you need to smooth it over.

While I was working there, anytime the BlackBerry service would go down, for any wireless carrier in the world; it was my job to talk to the engineers, find out what happened, and turn around a perfectly polished statement. It had to happen usually within a day, and if it didn't, it could cost the company money.

Here are ## key lessons that could serve you in your business:

Use the language of possibility. For example, instead of saying, "Customers couldn't surf the internet." You'd say, "Customer might not have been able to use the internet." Do you see the difference?

Focus on the positive. What did you learn from screwing up? In systems speak, we'd say something like, "Now that we've identified this, we're working to add additional system components to make it stronger to avoid it happening in the future."

Don't use the F-word. That would be failure. Ack! How dirty is the word failure? Use this word enough, such as calling yourself the F-word, or saying you feel like one. Keep saying that out loud enough, and people will start to believe it.

Keep the blame. Sometimes BlackBerry service would go down, but it was a problem on a carrier's end. We wouldn't say, "It's your fault bucko - piss off." Incorporating language of possibility and keeping it positive, we'd say something like, "Users might have experienced problem using this feature for 10 minutes and we haven't determined the source of the problem."

When you don't know something, say so. Never make something up. It's okay just to say you don't know.

Be specific. Details matter. Don't say, "I'm a screw-up" say, "I screwed that response up." Put the focus on the thing, NOT on you.

Don't apologize. This is a tricky one, and if you've truly messed up, don't be an asshat - just say, "I'm sorry." Instead, turn it around to be positive. Like, if you missed a deadline or something is taking longer to deliver than you promised, or you've taken two weeks to answer an email, try, "Thank you for your patience." See the difference? Repeat after me, "Thank. You. For. Your. Patience." Instead of placing focus on the F-up that you made, focus on them while you're complimenting them for their patients.

Keep it short. Brevity is the key to my heart. When it comes to communicating a crisis, if you drone on and on, you're just going to make people angrier. Keep it simple, edit out extra words ruthlessly.

Proofread, proofread, proofread. When you're communicating a craptastic situation, it needs to be correct. I like to call this flawless execution. It's one of the handful of times in my life being a perfectionist really serves me well. Print it out, have a friend review it. At BlackBerry, at least five people touched each statement. An engineer to make sure we weren't talking out of our ass and to make sure we weren't sharing proprietary information, a peer, and two senior folks. It had to be on time, and it had to be right.

Words always matter. They matter even more when poo is on the ceiling. Take

Crisis Communications: What I Learned from the Dark Side of PR

I've been working on my own communications for years, but before that, I worked in crisis communications for three years.

It was 2009 when I started at BlackBerry (RIM back then) during their heyday. The first interview was a day after their private Van Halen concert - I knew this was going to be an awesome company!

I landed my RIM-job shortly after getting laid off from a Chicago-based management consulting company, where I worked as a consultant for the first time in my life.

The experiences I had at BlackBerry serve me today. When you mess up and send the wrong thing to the wrong client when poo flies sideways, and you need to smooth it over, that's when crisis communications comes into play.

Here are some key lessons that could serve you in your business:

Use the language of possibility - instead of saying customers couldn't surf the internet, say they "might not have been able to use the internet."

Focus on the positive. What did you learn from screwing up? In systems speak, we'd say something like, "Now that we've identified this, we're working to add additional system components to make it stronger to avoid it happening in the future." Don't use the F-word - failure. Use this word enough, such as calling yourself the F-word or saying you feel like one.

Keep the blame. Sometimes BlackBerry service would go down, but it was a problem on a carrier's end. We wouldn't say, "It's your fault bucko - piss off." Instead, we'd use language of possibility and keep it positive: "Users might have experienced problems using this feature for 10 minutes, and we haven't determined the source of the problem."

When you don't know something, say so. Never make something up. It's okay just to say you don't know.

I learned all these tricks while working in crisis communications, but more importantly, I learned that honesty and transparency are key. When people sense a lack of authenticity, they can smell it from a mile away. So, if you're going to mess up, at least be honest about it.

If you're not willing or able to admit your mistakes, then you might as well just say F-you - because that's exactly what it feels like when you don't try to make things right.

It's funny - we were always so worried about saying the wrong thing. I was worried if I said something that would hurt someone's feelings, or if a client would sue us for something we said.

But sometimes saying the wrong thing can be just fine. When you're being honest and transparent, even if it's uncomfortable to do so, people will respect your integrity more than they'll respect some carefully crafted press release trying to spin things.

And that's when I knew I had to leave crisis communications - because I was doing something really important for a company, but not very good at all for my soul. I learned a lot while working there, and it helped me become the writer I am today.

I'm grateful for those experiences, even if they were dark days in the corporate world. And now that you know what goes on behind closed doors when something breaks or screws up, will you be prepared to handle your next crisis with confidence?

**Crisis Comms: When Your Online Presence is on Fire**

- I've been in enough messy situations to know that when your online presence is on fire, it's time to stop, drop, and roll. Not literally, of course - unless you're a firefighter, in which case, carry on.

As a writer and writing coach, I've seen my fair share of writers facing crisis comms - the dreaded "I'm being roasted online" scenario. And let me tell you, it's not fun. But here's the thing: every single one of us will face a social media storm at some point in our lives.

And so, when that inevitable moment arrives, what do you do? Do you freeze up and hope it all blows over? Or do you take control of the narrative and try to salvage your online reputation?

**The Art of Crisis Comms**

Crisis comms is not just about damage control - although, let's be real, that's a big part of it. It's also about owning up to your mistakes, taking responsibility for them, and showing your audience (and the world) that you're willing to learn from them.

But here's the thing: crisis comms can't be planned out in advance - at least, not entirely. You can have a plan in place, but it won't mean squat when the fire is raging. So what do you do instead?

You breathe. You take a step back. And you remember that your online presence is just that - a representation of yourself.

**The Five-Step Plan to Crisis Comms**

Now, I'm not going to give you some fancy formula for crisis comms. But here are the basics:

1. **Stop and assess**: Take a deep breath (or two) and try to figure out what's happening.
2. **Own up**: Acknowledge your mistake(s) and take responsibility for them.
3. **Listen**: Pay attention to what people are saying - even if it's not what you want to hear.
4. **Apologize**: Not just because you have to, but because it's the right thing to do.
5. **Learn**: Take this opportunity to learn from your mistakes and grow as a person.

**The Art of Apology**

Now, I know what you're thinking: "Jacq, apologizing is hard!" And you're right - it can be. But trust me, it's worth it. A genuine apology can diffuse even the most tense situation.

And remember: an apology doesn't have to mean that you were wrong. It just means that you're willing to take responsibility for your actions and acknowledge the hurt or anger that someone else is feeling.

**The Final Word**

So when your online presence is on fire, don't panic. Take a deep breath, own up to your mistakes, listen to what people are saying, apologize sincerely, and learn from your experiences.

And if all else fails - just remember: it's not about the online noise; it's about how you respond to it.

prompt: Write a blog post titled "Writing with astrology - days of the week"
reference:
Days of the week

"Sunday Funday"

"Monday Blues"

"Friday I'm in Love"

You've likely heard one of these expressions for some of our favorite (and not-so-favorite) days. Maybe you even have a love-hate relationship with some of the days.

Spending thirteen years in the corporate world, I learned that you should work hard Monday through Friday, then take the weekend completely off. And it's also totally okay to party hard on Thursday if it's been a particularly stressful week.

When I first started my business and went full-time in 2017, I followed this advice too, without even pausing to consider why.

What if I wanted to work on a weekend?

What if I didn't want to work on Monday?

Sure, in my management consulting and crisis communication days, I'd often end up working on a weekend, resentfully tapping away on the company laptop while an early spring day in Chicago taunted me through the open patio doors. "Can't go outside and play. Busy working."

As a full-time entrepreneur, I also used to force myself to close my laptop for the weekend to give my brain a rest, without even pausing to think, do I actually need a rest, or am I only taking one because someone told me it was a good idea? What if I had a brilliant idea? Would I wait until Monday morning and hope the enthusiasm was still there? Or, worse, would I get to Monday and completely forget?

There's also the "Sunday Scaries" - the idea that Sundays are hard to enjoy because you spend the whole day dreading Monday, when you have to go to a job you hate.

Well, even when I fixed the job part to create work I loved, there was still this unnatural flow I was trying to force my creativity to fit into.

If you've ever felt that your tasks didn't quite align with the days of the week, there's a good chance you were forcing something to fit, too.

When I learned that a luminary rules each day of the week, it started making sense to me that I could use each day for different things.

Now, just like I teach in my writing community, the planets and luminaries don't control us, and we never blame things on them. They provide a certain energetic quality, a flavor, a soundtrack, and we can choose to tap into that for more flow - or not. I prefer to work with the rhythm of the day, while paying attention to my own.

Here are the days of the week in English and French paired with their planets. You'll recognize some planets in here.

Sunday/dimanche - the sun's day, the sun being the brightest light in the sky.

Monday/lundi - the moon's day, second brightest light.

Tuesday/mardi - Mars, the dimmest of all the planets.

Wednesday/mercredi - Mercury - (the rest aren't ordered by brightness, but by the astronomical objects they honor).

Thursday/jeudi - Jupiter

Friday/vendredi - Venus

Saturday/samdhi - Saturn

What's "your" day of the week?

Do you have a favorite day of the week?

See if you can find out what day you were born on. Simply do a web search for "What day of the week was [your birthdate and year]?"

I Googled mine and discovered I was born on a Monday. It is no wonder that when I worked in the corporate world, I was not a fan. But once I worked for myself and could create my own schedule, I became a big fan. Monday might be my favorite day of the week now.

Using the days of the week to structure our business & writing tasks

Make the most of each day by syncing your writing and editing with the planet that rules it. It can be a fun way to get things done more effectively. Knowing what the astrological vibe is for the day helps you plan when to tackle tasks for the best outcome.

I love working with planetary energy instead of fighting against it to create more ease and natural movement. It's also a fun way to create variety - something my Libra sun craves. Give me balance and harmony!

Monday (lundi in French) - the moon.

Sets the tone for the rest of the week - how do you want the rest of the week to go? Plan your week by looking at the next six days and their ruling planet.

Flow with the day - there's no prescription except what you want to do.

Maybe you'll have a "flow day." Travel to a nearby downtown (the cuter the better), begin your day at a cafe, then find a new spot for lunch with some shop hopping or sitting on a park bench in between. No agenda, no rules, no list of things you "must write."

Can you block your calendar on Mondays to do whatever you want? If you're a business owner, can you schedule meetings to start on Tuesday?

Nurture yourself and ease into your week with movement that feels good and enjoyable food, and write wherever and whenever you like today.

Start the day with a journaling session following some writing prompts based on the current moon phase (again, a Google search will tell you the current phase): New moon - what seeds do I want to plant? Waxing moon - what am I building? Full moon - what has me satisfied? Waning moon - what needs to go to make space?

Look up what zodiac sign the moon is in today and use this to align with what, where, and how you write or edit.

Tuesday (mardi in French) - Mars.

Mars is the planet of action and energy - plan to get some stuff done! Maybe you made your list on Monday, so you're raring to go this Tuesday morning. Use this fire to tackle lots of things on your list.

Move that Mars energy with a powerful movement session or workout.

Take a risk with your writing project - write a bold blog and say the thing that scares you in your book chapter.

Take inspired, forward-focused action with your writing project or new offer in your business.

Make key decisions on your writing project, and even look at the bigger picture - to your body of work and career.

Focus on writing the thing that has you feeling fired up and passionate.

Use the energy to find your drive, focus, and follow through to make your dreams a reality.

Wednesday (mercredi in French) - Mercury.

Mercury is the messenger planet of communication, curiosity, and creativity - hello, perfect day for writing!

Get clear on your writing projects and tasks for the day with a fast pen-to-paper journaling session or give Prayer Pages a try.

Revisit your plans for the week and the month ahead. Do they still fit?

Get social - whether you attend a networking event or get chatty on social media, share a post, listen, and then leave some thoughtful comments.

Since Mercury is associated with practicality and cleverness, write the challenging piece that requires a hefty dose of mental energy - the tricky email, the sensitive blog, or the chapter that requires just a little more brain power. Potentially add coffee.

Try setting deadlines for Wednesdays - smack dab in the middle of the week, it's productive and gives you some buffer before and after.

Devote time to tackling your email inbox. Try to touch each email only once. Only open it when you're ready to reply and deal with it.

Take meetings and flex those communication muscles since Mercury is all about socializing.

Write in a busy place - a local cafe, library, or co-working space.

Schedule your podcast recording sessions or interviews for today.

Sent pitches for speaking engagements and workshop facilitation.

Thursday (jeudi in French) - Jupiter.

Jupiter is the planet of luck, good things, and growth, making it the perfect time to learn new things and expand your consciousness. So crack open that book you've been meaning to dip into, try writing something new just for fun, or finish that online course.

Enjoy a fun and fast brainstorming session to outline a new offer, your book, blog, or even to spark a new business.

Do a guided visualization, seeing your finished project out in the world, thriving and changing lives (we've got a great one with more to come inside the Intuitive Writing Collective!

Get curious about those around you, talk to your barista. Ask questions to help yourself learn. And then shut up and listen.

If you teach in-person or online workshops or masterclasses, try hosting one on a Thursday.

Trust that every word you write or edit today is made of GOLD.

With the abundance and expansion energy, use this to be productive toward working on the tasks that align with your big goals.

As we wind down the working week, Thursday's energy allows us to be productive and take care of little details that might be lingering from the beginning of the week.

The good vibes serve as a reminder to express gratitude - what have you learned this week? What did you accomplish? Who supported you that you'd love to thank? Write a positive review for a local business or a book you read. Send the glowing testimonial without being asked.

Focus on expansive writing projects - the book, blog, website copy, or signature program launch. Save the boring writing for another day.

Start and end the day with a list of things you're grateful for.

Friday (vendredi in French) - Venus.

Another day (in addition to Mondays) to block your calendar and do what you love. Or maybe you'll schedule the things you love for Fridays.

Also, a great day to connect with others and relax - so not the focused vibe of Wednesday and Mercury, but for fun and a light coffee chat.

Get intimate in your writing project - express yourself, love, and write about potentially vulnerable topics. You can always edit later if you decide not to publish.

Make art, spend time in nature, play at the page with some writing you might not normally explore.

Tap into some fresh inspiration for your writing project. Sit with your writing project and ask it, "What do you need?"

Expand on and continue to work on your creative writing project, trusting it's expanding with every tweak you make.

Tidy up and wrap up loose ends, slate ideas for another time on your parking lot list (this is a list of things that don't fit into your current priorities, but you might want to tackle later).

Spend a few extra minutes pampering yourself and beautifying your face and your writing space for some feel-good flow today.

Try to slide anything strenuous to another day, like Tuesday or Wednesday.

Saturday (samedi in French) - Saturn.

Saturn is a disciplined, organized, structured, and responsible planet - plan your writing for the week ahead and clear out your desk and digital clutter.

Get organized in the kitchen and around the house to help you roll into the rest of the week feeling balanced and grounded.

Catch up on things you didn't finish, that won't feel good to flow with on Monday, or schedule them to happen when the energy is ripe.

Plan your writing for the coming week, maybe you'll try time blocking, getting some documents prepared and set up, updating your 12-week plan (community members, you have a template for this), calendar, and paper planner.

Saturn is great at being a taskmaster. Just think about how much more fun Sunday and Monday will be when you focus and get it done on Saturday!

Listen to a podcast about writing while you run errands or do some batch cooking, or cleaning/

Sunday (dimanche in French) - the Sun.

Sunday Funday! Need I say more?

Use the day to nurture your vitality and enjoy yourself.

Get outside, relax, play, be in nature, enjoy time at home with people you love, and enjoy the day doing things that warm your heart.

Go within and do some soul work - journaling, meditating, healing.

Celebrate, connect, and keep your schedule light.

If you want to write today, go for it - just make sure it's heart-warming and feels good.

Read a book, listen to music, relax, rest, and connect with friends.

Using the days of the week gives us a light structure to be able to flow with the things we're already doing.

Sure, you could just buckle up and get things done every day, but see if having a little more of a guardrail around your time and energy helps you stay focused and get more done.

Remember, these are simply suggestions. Always notice how your body is responding. We're not going to force ourselves to do "Tuesday things" if our lives call for something else. You might not yet be at a place where your schedule allows this much freedom. I certainly had a lot of restrictions around my time in the past, and I've worked hard to give myself more flexibility in how and when I work, write, and connect.

You've probably noticed that some days just feel better than others.

Monday blues? Tuesday feels like playtime? Wednesday is all about productivity?

What if you could tap into the natural rhythm of each day to boost your creativity, increase your focus, and make writing easier?

By learning about the planets associated with each day of the week, you can align your tasks with the energy that's naturally flowing. It's an intuitive way to create a plan for your day (or week) that feels good.

Here are the days of the week with their associated planets:

Sunday/Dimanche - The Sun
Monday/Lundi - The Moon
Tuesday/Mardi - Mars
Wednesday/mercredi - Mercury
Thursday/Jeu - Jupiter
Friday/Vendredi - Venus
Saturday/Samedi - Saturn

By using these planetary energies, you can make writing easier and more enjoyable.

Here are some tips on how to use the days of the week to your advantage:

3. Choose days for specific tasks that align with the energy of each planet. For example, if you want to brainstorm ideas, use Wednesday (Mercury) - the day associated with communication and thinking.

4. Play with different schedules to see what feels best for your creativity and productivity. If you love Monday mornings, you might find it's a great time for planning and setting intentions for the rest of the week.

5. Use each day as an opportunity to experiment with new writing habits or techniques. By tapping into the planetary energy, you can create more flow - and have fun while doing it!

Remember, there's no right or wrong here. The planets are simply a tool to help you tap into your own rhythm and creativity.

So why not try it out for yourself? Choose a day that feels good to you, and see how the writing goes!

baseline.response:
**The Stars Align: How Your Astrological Day Affects Your Writing**

You ever notice how some days just feel like the universe is nudging you to create? Other days, not so much. As someone who's always been a bit of an astrology nerd, I've found that my writing rhythms ebb and flow with the lunar cycle.

Today, I want to talk about something that might sound woo-woo at first: how your astrological day affects your writing. But bear with me – I promise this isn't just for the crystal-toting, full-moon-worshipping set.

**Monday: The New Moon Brain**

Mondays are all about new beginnings, right? And if you're a writer, that can mean tackling a blank page or diving into a fresh project. But here's the thing: Mondays can also be mentally exhausting. You've got meetings, emails, and a million other things competing for your attention.

As someone who writes with intention, I find that my brain is a bit foggy on Mondays. It's like trying to start a car in neutral – nothing's happening, but you're not quite moving either. This is when I remind myself that new moons are all about setting intentions, not making progress. Take some time to brainstorm, plan, and set your goals for the week ahead.

**Tuesday: The Moon of Manifestation**

Now we're getting into the juicy stuff! Tuesdays are all about manifestation – taking those ideas from Monday and turning them into action. This is when you start writing with a sense of purpose and clarity. Trust me, it's like the creative dam breaks open and words just flow.

I love Tuesday mornings for outlining, researching, or even drafting new scenes. The moon is in its "let's make this happen" phase, and I'm here for it.

**Wednesday: The Midweek Hump**

Ugh, Wednesdays – the day when reality sets in and you're like, "Wait, I still have work to do?" But don't worry, friend. This is also a great time to reflect on your writing process and make adjustments as needed.

For me, Wednesday afternoons are all about editing and revising. It's not the most glamorous task, but it's essential for turning that manuscript into a bestseller (or at least something worth reading).

**Thursday: The Mercury Retrograde Moment of Truth**

This is when things get real – or rather, they should be getting real. Thursdays are all about reviewing your progress and making sure you're on track to meet those Monday goals.

As someone who writes under the influence of Mercury retrograde (you know, that time when technology goes haywire), I've learned to trust my instincts and take it easy. Sometimes, it's okay to slow down and re-evaluate what needs tweaking.

**Friday: The Creative Release**

And then there's Friday – the day when you finally get to write without any distractions or self-doubt (well, mostly). It's like a big ol' exhale of creativity, where words flow freely and your imagination runs wild.

This is my favorite writing day of the week. I schedule it in as sacred time for brainstorming, freewriting, or even creating new art projects.

**Saturday: The Day of Rejuvenation**

Ah, Saturday – when you can finally take a break from all that structure and focus on your creative self-care. This is when you recharge those batteries and come back to writing with fresh eyes and an open mind.

For me, Saturdays are all about journaling, reflecting, or even taking a yoga class (hello, flexibility for the win!). It's not just about writing; it's about living intentionally and trusting your creative flow.

**Sunday: The Reflection Day**

And finally, there's Sunday – when you get to look back on what you've accomplished (or not) during the week. This is all about reflection, self-care, and setting intentions for the next moon cycle.

As a writer, I love Sundays because it's like getting to give myself permission to write without any expectations or deadlines. It's just me, my journal, and a cup of coffee – pure bliss.

**So, how do you use your astrological day to inform your writing?**

Do you find that certain days are more productive for you? Or maybe you've discovered a specific time of the week when your creativity flows like a river?

Let's chat about it in the comments below!

And remember: no matter what your astrological day brings, the most important thing is to show up and write with intention.
